{"id":"project-LICHEN-t0cg","title":"SLIP decode() merges multiple frames when END appears mid-stream","description":"File: python/src/lichen/slip/codec.py
Lines: 66-68

The decode() function's docstring says: 'frame may include leading/trailing END bytes; they are ignored.'

However, the implementation treats ALL END bytes the same, including those in the middle:

```python
if b == END:
    i += 1
    continue
```

This means if you pass two concatenated frames, they get merged:

Example:
```python
decode(b'\xc0\x41\xc0\x42\xc0')
# Returns: b'\x41\x42' (WRONG - merged two packets)
# Expected: Either return b'\x41' (first frame) or raise error
```

Per RFC 1055, END marks packet boundaries. A middle END should either:
1. Stop decoding and return just the first frame
2. Raise an error indicating multiple frames were passed

Current behavior silently merges frames, causing data corruption when the caller passes concatenated frames.

Fix: Stop at first END after seeing any data, or raise ValueError on unexpected middle END.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"me@mark.atwood.name","created_at":"2026-07-12T06:27:46.584249Z","created_by":"Mark Atwood","updated_at":"2026-07-12T06:27:46.584276Z"}
